quick_glance: The ancient kingdom of the Nile valley, one of the most powerful empires of the ancient world; the land of Israel's bondage and the setting of the Exodus.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the empire/kingdom; see EGYPTIANS for the people group

quick_glance: A great and powerful empire on the Tigris River that conquered the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC and carried its people into captivity.
The empire centered on the city of Babylon that conquered Judah in 586 BC, destroyed the Temple at Jerusalem, and carried the Jews into exile.
The empire founded by Cyrus the Great that conquered Babylon and permitted the Jews to return to Jerusalem and rebuild the Temple.
The empire of Alexander the Great that conquered Persia and spread Greek language and culture across the ancient Near East, forming the cultural backdrop of the New Testament.
disambiguation_note: Refers to Alexander's empire; see GREEKS for the broader people group

quick_glance: The Greek dynasty that ruled Egypt after Alexander the Great's death; they controlled Judea from 301 to 198 BC and commissioned the Septuagint translation of the Hebrew Scriptures.
disambiguation_note: The Greek-ruled successor kingdom in Egypt, distinct from the ancient Egyptian Empire

quick_glance: The Greek dynasty that ruled Syria and Mesopotamia after Alexander the Great; under Antiochus IV Epiphanes they desecrated the Jerusalem Temple, provoking the Maccabean revolt.
disambiguation_note: Distinct from the broader SYRIANS entry

quick_glance: The dominant world power of the New Testament era, Rome ruled Judea from 63 BC and provided the political and legal context for the life of Jesus and the early church.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the empire; see ROMANS for the people group

quick_glance: The general name for the inhabitants of the land of Canaan before the Israelite conquest; descended from Canaan the son of Ham according to Genesis.
Descendants of Heth, the son of Canaan; they dwelt in Hebron and its vicinity and are listed among the nations dispossessed by Israel.
disambiguation_note: Biblical Hittites are distinct from the Anatolian Hittite Empire, though a connection is debated

quick_glance: A powerful Canaanite people who occupied the hill country on both sides of the Jordan; their kings Sihon and Og were defeated by Israel under Moses.
One of the Canaanite peoples dwelling in the land before the Israelite conquest, frequently listed among the nations to be dispossessed.
A Canaanite people descended from Canaan; the Gibeonites who deceived Joshua were Hivites, and they also dwelt near Mount Hermon.
A Canaanite people who held the fortress of Jebus (later Jerusalem) until David captured it and made it his capital city.
One of the seven Canaanite nations listed as inhabiting the Promised Land before Israel's conquest; little is known of them beyond their inclusion in the standard lists.
A non-Semitic people who occupied the maritime plain of Canaan and were frequent enemies of Israel from the period of the Judges through the monarchy.
A seafaring Canaanite people dwelling along the coast of modern Lebanon; their chief cities were Tyre and Sidon, and they supplied materials for Solomon's Temple.
Descendants of Moab, the son of Lot; they occupied the territory east of the Dead Sea and were frequently at war with Israel, though Ruth the Moabitess became an ancestress of David.
Descendants of Esau, the brother of Jacob; they inhabited the region south of the Dead Sea and were perpetual rivals of Israel despite their kinship.
disambiguation_note: Later known as Idumeans in the Greco-Roman period; see IDUMEANS

quick_glance: Descendants of Ben-ammi, the son of Lot; they occupied the territory east of the Jordan near the Jabbok and were persistent enemies of Israel.
A nomadic people descended from Esau who attacked Israel at Rephidim after the Exodus; they were declared perpetual enemies of the Lord and eventually destroyed.
Descendants of Midian, the son of Abraham by Keturah; they were desert traders who oppressed Israel in the time of the Judges until defeated by Gideon.
A Semitic people who established kingdoms in Syria and Mesopotamia; the kingdom of Aram-Damascus was a major rival of the northern kingdom of Israel.
disambiguation_note: Often translated 'Syrians' in the KJV; see SYRIANS

quick_glance: The KJV translation for the Aramean peoples; Syria in the Bible generally refers to the region of Aram centered on Damascus.
disambiguation_note: The biblical term 'Syrians' usually refers to Arameans; distinct from SELEUCID_SYRIA

quick_glance: The covenant people descended from the twelve sons of Jacob (Israel), chosen by God and delivered from Egypt to inherit the Promised Land.
The leading tribe of Israel, descended from Jacob's fourth son Judah; the royal tribe from which David and ultimately the Messiah came.
The priestly tribe of Israel, descended from Jacob's third son Levi; set apart for the service of the tabernacle and Temple, they received no territorial inheritance.
disambiguation_note: The tribe as a whole; see LEVITES for the non-priestly servants and PRIESTS_OF_ISRAEL for the Aaronic priests

quick_glance: A name applied to the Israelites, especially in their relations with foreigners; derived either from Eber the ancestor of Abraham or from a word meaning 'those from beyond' the river.
The twelve divisions of the Israelite nation descended from the twelve sons of Jacob, each allotted territory in the Promised Land except the tribe of Levi.
A mixed people descended from Israelites left in the northern kingdom and foreign colonists settled there by Assyria; they worshipped at Mount Gerizim and were despised by the Jews.
An Arab people who controlled trade routes south and east of Judea, with their capital at Petra; their king Aretas IV is mentioned in Paul's escape from Damascus.

The Greco-Roman name for the Edomites, who by the intertestamental period had migrated into southern Judea; they were forcibly converted to Judaism under John Hyrcanus and produced the Herodian dynasty.
disambiguation_note: The later form of the EDOMITES in the Greco-Roman period

quick_glance: A Jewish political faction that advocated violent resistance to Roman rule; Simon the Zealot was one of the twelve apostles.
A Jewish ascetic sect of the Second Temple period who lived in communal settings, practiced ritual purity, and are generally associated with the Dead Sea Scrolls community at Qumran.
disambiguation_note: Not directly named in Scripture but described by Josephus and Philo; included for historical context

quick_glance: An extremist splinter group of the Zealots who carried concealed daggers and assassinated Roman sympathizers; mentioned in Acts when the Roman tribune mistook Paul for their leader.

citations.excerpt: Art not thou that Egyptian, which before these days madest an uproar, and leddest out into the wilderness four thousand men that were murderers?

quick_glance: A Jewish religious party known for strict observance of the Mosaic law and the oral tradition; bitterly opposed by the Sadducees in their claim that the oral tradition was as binding as the written law.
A Jewish religious party composed mainly of the priestly aristocracy who accepted only the written Torah, denied the resurrection of the dead, and controlled the Temple worship.
Professional interpreters and copyists of the Mosaic law who exercised great influence in Jewish religious life; frequently mentioned alongside the Pharisees in the Gospels.
A Jewish political party that supported the Herodian dynasty and Roman rule; they joined with the Pharisees in seeking to entrap Jesus.
The supreme religious office in Israel held by descendants of Aaron; the high priest alone entered the Holy of Holies on the Day of Atonement and presided over the Sanhedrin.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the office and its holders as a group; see PRIESTS_OF_ISRAEL for the broader priesthood

quick_glance: The Jewish ruling dynasty descended from the Maccabees who governed an independent Judea from 140 to 63 BC, combining the offices of high priest and king.
The Jewish family led by Judas Maccabeus who revolted against the Seleucid king Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC, purified the Temple, and established Jewish independence.
The Greek-speaking rulers and settlers under the Seleucid dynasty who imposed Hellenistic culture on Judea, provoking the Maccabean revolt when Antiochus IV desecrated the Temple.
disambiguation_note: The people and cultural influence, as distinct from the SELEUCID_SYRIA political entity

quick_glance: The citizens and subjects of Rome who appear throughout the New Testament as soldiers, officials, and the governing authority over Judea; Paul claimed Roman citizenship.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the people; see ROMAN_EMPIRE for the political entity

quick_glance: In the New Testament, 'Greeks' refers broadly to Gentiles of Greek language and culture; the spread of Hellenistic culture provided the common language in which the New Testament was written.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the people and culture; see MACEDONIAN_GREEK for Alexander's empire

quick_glance: The people of Macedonia in northern Greece; Paul received his vision of the 'man of Macedonia' and established churches at Philippi, Thessalonica, and Berea in this region.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the NT-era people and region; see MACEDONIAN_GREEK for Alexander's empire

quick_glance: An Iranian people who ruled a vast empire east of the Roman Empire; Parthians are listed among those present at Pentecost in Jerusalem.
Descendants of Cush, the son of Ham; they inhabited the region south of Egypt known as Ethiopia or Nubia, and appear in Scripture from Genesis through the prophets.
disambiguation_note: Biblical 'Ethiopia' refers to Nubia/Cush, not modern Ethiopia

quick_glance: The people of Egypt who appear throughout Scripture as hosts, oppressors, and neighbors of Israel; their culture profoundly influenced the Israelites during the sojourn and Exodus.
disambiguation_note: Refers to the people; see EGYPTIAN_EMPIRE for the political entity

quick_glance: The peoples of the Arabian peninsula mentioned in Scripture as traders and desert dwellers; they appear from the time of Solomon through the New Testament at Pentecost.
Descendants of Ishmael, the son of Abraham and Hagar; they were nomadic traders who inhabited the desert regions east of Canaan and are identified with the caravan that carried Joseph to Egypt.
A nomadic Midianite clan of metalworkers; Moses' father-in-law Jethro was a Kenite, and they maintained a friendly relationship with Israel, settling in Judah's territory.
A Kenite clan descended from Jonadab son of Rechab who vowed to abstain from wine, agriculture, and permanent dwellings; God commended their faithfulness through Jeremiah.
A class of Temple servants, 'the given ones,' assigned to assist the Levites in the service of the sanctuary; they returned from the Babylonian exile with Ezra and Nehemiah.
Gentile converts to Judaism who submitted to circumcision, baptism, and the Mosaic law; they appear in the New Testament as worshippers in synagogues and among early Christians.
A term used in Scripture for all peoples and nations who are not Israelites; the inclusion of Gentiles in God's salvation plan is a central theme of the New Testament.
Jews living outside the land of Israel, scattered among the nations from the Babylonian exile onward; their synagogues across the Roman Empire became launching points for Paul's mission.
Members of the tribe of Levi who were set apart for service in the tabernacle and Temple but were not priests; they served as musicians, gatekeepers, teachers, and assistants.
disambiguation_note: Non-priestly members of the tribe of Levi; see PRIESTS_OF_ISRAEL for the Aaronic priesthood

quick_glance: Descendants of Aaron set apart to offer sacrifices, burn incense, and minister before the Lord in the tabernacle and Temple; they formed a distinct order within the tribe of Levi.
disambiguation_note: The general priesthood; see HIGH_PRIESTS for the supreme office

quick_glance: The supreme Jewish council of seventy-one elders that governed religious and civil affairs in Judea; it tried Jesus and later the apostles Peter and Paul.
